title: Hazel woven garden panel
subtitle: A traditional hurdle panel woven from hazel rods for paths, beds, and screens
excerpt: A woven hazel hurdle is the standard low border for a kitchen garden path or raised bed: sturdy enough to hold back soil and edging mulch, natural-looking, and biodegradable when it finally breaks down after three to four seasons. Hazel rods cut in winter and woven green make a tight panel without any fixings; the rods dry into each other and hold without movement. This beginner project covers setting the uprights and weaving the panel.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for the hazel hurdle tradition. Encyclopaedia Britannica 11th edition (1911) for coppice management and hurdle-making in the English countryside.

glossaryTerms.definition: A portable woven panel of hazel or willow rods used for fencing, windbreaks, and garden edging. Traditionally made by the hurdle-maker or 'hurdler' from locally coppiced hazel.
The heavy upright stake of a hurdle, driven into the ground. Sales are from the thicker, older coppice poles; the thinner weaving rods are twisted between them.
Freshly cut timber at high moisture content. Hazel rods used within a few days of cutting weave without cracking and dry into each other, tightening the panel.

content.text: is the traditional British sheep-hurdle and garden-panel form, made from a winter coppice cut while the hazel is still

and flexible. Thicker poles are set as uprights; thinner rods are woven between them in an over-under pattern. As the rods dry, they tighten against the uprights and the panel becomes a rigid structure without nails or wire. A low garden hurdle 30 to 40 cm tall is suitable as a raised-bed edge or path border. Taller panels (90 cm to 120 cm) serve as windbreaks or screens.
For a low garden panel 90 cm long by 35 cm tall: 5 upright

(hazel poles 20 to 25 mm diameter, 70 cm long so 35 cm goes in the ground and 35 cm stands above), and 20 to 30 weaving rods (hazel or willow, 8 to 12 mm diameter, 100 to 110 cm long). Cut all from the winter coppice and use within 5 days.
A billhook to cut poles to length and point the sales. A sloyd knife for fine trimming.
Billhook cuts away from the body; the free hand holds the pole above and behind the strike zone.

Point the base of each sale with two angled billhook cuts. Drive the sales into the ground at equal intervals (about 20 to 22 cm spacing for a 90 cm panel) to a depth of 35 cm. Drive with a mallet rather than a hammer to avoid splitting the top of the sale. The row of sales should be straight and vertical.

Start the first weaving rod at one end sale. Weave: in front of the first sale, behind the second, in front of the third, behind the fourth, in front of the fifth. Push the butt end of the rod into the ground on the outside of the first sale to anchor it. Bring the tip end around the outside of the last sale and tuck it back in the other direction.

Add each new rod starting from the same end, but reversing the over-under sequence (so if the previous rod went in front of the first sale, this one goes behind it). This alternating sequence locks each row against the previous. Twist any rod that shows signs of cracking as it bends; a quarter-twist releases the tension in the fibres and allows a tighter bend. Pack each row down firmly against the previous one by hand. Continue until the panel is the target height.

The top of the panel is finished by threading the final two or three weaving rods back through the panel on the same weave line, each one locking the one below. Trim all projecting rod ends flush with the billhook or sloyd knife.
A taller screen panel (90 to 120 cm above ground) needs sales 180 cm long (90 cm driven in, 90 cm above) and additional sales at 15 cm spacing rather than 20 cm to prevent the screen bowing in wind.
Willow rods weave more easily than hazel for curved panels; their longer and more flexible rods can be bent around a curved row of sales to form a circular raised bed surround.

No maintenance needed. Replace individual sales if they rot at soil level without dismantling the panel; new sales can be driven in next to the rotted ones. A well-made hazel panel lasts three to four seasons above ground.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Problems weaving a hazel garden panel.

items.symptom: Weaving rods crack rather than bending around the sales
items.cause: The rods are too old from the cut (more than 5 days) or the diameter is too large for the tight curve needed.
items.fix: Use freshly cut rods within 48 hours. Rods over 15 mm diameter will crack at tight bends; use thinner ones for the weave.

items.symptom: The panel bows forward between the sales
items.cause: The sales are too widely spaced for the rod diameter being used.
items.fix: Add an extra sale in the centre. For future panels, space sales at 15 to 18 cm rather than 20 cm when using thin weaving rods.
